title: Non-Overlapping Intervals
description:
## Non-Overlapping Intervals

Given an array of intervals `intervals` where `intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i]`, return the minimum number of intervals you must remove to make the rest of the intervals non-overlapping.

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= intervals.length <= 10^5`
- `intervals[i].length == 2`
- `-5 * 10^4 <= start_i < end_i <= 5 * 10^4`

examples.explanation: Remove [1,3] so the rest don't overlap.
Remove two of the three [1,2] intervals.
They don't overlap (touching at 2 is okay).

hiddenCases.explanation: [1,100] is the problematic long interval that overlaps the others
Remove one from each overlapping pair

solutions.label: Greedy (Sort by End) — O(n log n)
solutions.explanation: Sort by end time. Greedily keep intervals that don't overlap with the last kept interval — always prefer the interval that ends earliest (smallest end time), as it leaves the most room for future intervals. When there's an overlap, remove the current interval (which ends later). This greedy strategy maximizes the number of non-overlapping intervals we keep, minimizing removals.

solutions.label: Greedy (Sort by Start) — O(n log n)
solutions.explanation: Sort by start time. When there's an overlap (current start < previous end), we must remove one: remove the one that ends later (keep `min(prev_end, end)`) to minimize future conflicts. This is equivalent to the end-sort greedy but reasons about the start.

hints: Minimizing removals is equivalent to maximizing the number of intervals you can keep. Think about which intervals to keep.
Sort by end time and greedily keep intervals that don't overlap. The interval ending earliest is always the better choice to keep.
